Watkinson /Guy /Hadgraft /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:225–230 226 cinolone acetonide permeability in human skin, Ostren- ga et al. [8] observed that pre-treatment of skin with pure PG caused the skin barrier to become less pliable and at- tributed this effect to dehydration. Zatz and Dalvi [9] in- vestigated benzocaine permeation in hairless mouse skin from a range of solvents and showed that, with water as a standard, interaction effects were not significant when PG was used as a solvent. Hoelgaard and Mollgaard [10] examined the effects of PG on metronidazole flux en- hancement in human skin and suggested a possible ‘car- rier-solvent’ effect. Kasting et al. [11] demonstrated sig- nificant permeation enhancement of triprolidine base from pure PG when compared with oil vehicles in human skin. These workers related this direct effect of PG on the membrane to solvent incorporation in the stratum cor- neum in a sufficient quantity to modify permeant solu- bility in the tissue. As demonstrated previously, topical presentations of agents like ibuprofen which are intended for subcutane- ous action cannot be assumed to be pharmaceutically and clinically equivalent or indeed interchangeable [12] . Moreover, although it is evident that different penetra- tion enhancers influence drug diffusion characteristics to different extents [13] , the complexity of most pharma- ceutical preparations makes it difficult to deconvolve the exact influence of specific enhancers on drug perme- ation. Recently we reported the results of a systematic in- vestigation of the influence of binary systems of ethanol and water on ibuprofen solubility and in vitro permeation characteristics in model membranes and human skin [14] . Analysis of the data confirmed that increasing etha- nol content enhanced the solubility of ibuprofen in both membranes as well as in the vehicle itself. The aim of this work was to extend these studies to explore the influence of PG on ibuprofen permeation and to investigate wheth- er specific effects on partition and diffusion parameters may be distinguished. A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was then diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was performed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml min –1 . The retention time was approximately 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; coefficients of variation were ! 5% in all experi- ments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was obtained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [15] . It was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was then immersed in cold water to aid mounting onto filter paper and stored in a freezer until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . (1) 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h    d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±    where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between parameters were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05.

Watkinson /Guy /Hadgraft /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:225–230 226 cinolone acetonide permeability in human skin, Ostren- ga et al. [8] observed that pre-treatment of skin with pure PG caused the skin barrier to become less pliable and at- tributed this effect to dehydration. Zatz and Dalvi [9] in- vestigated benzocaine permeation in hairless mouse skin from a range of solvents and showed that, with water as a standard, interaction effects were not significant when PG was used as a solvent. Hoelgaard and Mollgaard [10] examined the effects of PG on metronidazole flux en- hancement in human skin and suggested a possible ‘car- rier-solvent’ effect. Kasting et al. [11] demonstrated sig- nificant permeation enhancement of triprolidine base from pure PG when compared with oil vehicles in human skin. These workers related this direct effect of PG on the membrane to solvent incorporation in the stratum cor- neum in a sufficient quantity to modify permeant solu- bility in the tissue. As demonstrated previously, topical presentations of agents like ibuprofen which are intended for subcutane- ous action cannot be assumed to be pharmaceutically and clinically equivalent or indeed interchangeable [12] . Moreover, although it is evident that different penetra- tion enhancers influence drug diffusion characteristics to different extents [13] , the complexity of most pharma- ceutical preparations makes it difficult to deconvolve the exact influence of specific enhancers on drug perme- ation. Recently we reported the results of a systematic in- vestigation of the influence of binary systems of ethanol and water on ibuprofen solubility and in vitro permeation characteristics in model membranes and human skin [14] . Analysis of the data confirmed that increasing etha- nol content enhanced the solubility of ibuprofen in both membranes as well as in the vehicle itself. A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems. Watkinson /Guy /Hadgraft /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:225–230 226 cinolone acetonide permeability in human skin, Ostren- ga et al. [8] observed that pre-treatment of skin with pure PG caused the skin barrier to become less pliable and at- tributed this effect to dehydration. Zatz and Dalvi [9] in- vestigated benzocaine permeation in hairless mouse skin from a range of solvents and showed that, with water as a standard, interaction effects were not significant when PG was used as a solvent. Hoelgaard and Mollgaard [10] examined the effects of PG on metronidazole flux en- hancement in human skin and suggested a possible ‘car- rier-solvent’ effect. Kasting et al. [11] demonstrated sig- nificant permeation enhancement of triprolidine base from pure PG when compared with oil vehicles in human skin. These workers related this direct effect of PG on the membrane to solvent incorporation in the stratum cor- neum in a sufficient quantity to modify permeant solu- bility in the tissue. As demonstrated previously, topical presentations of agents like ibuprofen which are intended for subcutane- ous action cannot be assumed to be pharmaceutically and clinically equivalent or indeed interchangeable [12] . Moreover, although it is evident that different penetra- tion enhancers influence drug diffusion characteristics to different extents [13] , the complexity of most pharma- ceutical preparations makes it difficult to deconvolve the exact influence of specific enhancers on drug perme- ation. Recently we reported the results of a systematic in- vestigation of the influence of binary systems of ethanol and water on ibuprofen solubility and in vitro permeation characteristics in model membranes and human skin [14] . Analysis of the data confirmed that increasing etha- nol content enhanced the solubility of ibuprofen in both membranes as well as in the vehicle itself. The aim of this work was to extend these studies to explore the influence of PG on ibuprofen permeation and to investigate wheth- er specific effects on partition and diffusion parameters may be distinguished. A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was then diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was performed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml min –1 . The retention time was approximately 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; coefficients of variation were ! 5% in all experi- ments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was obtained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [15] . It was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was then immersed in cold water to aid mounting onto filter paper and stored in a freezer until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . (1) 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h    d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±    where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between parameters were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05.

The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [15] . It was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was carefully peeled away from the dermis. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . Watkinson /Guy /Hadgraft /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:225–230 226 cinolone acetonide permeability in human skin, Ostren- ga et al. [8] observed that pre-treatment of skin with pure PG caused the skin barrier to become less pliable and at- tributed this effect to dehydration. Zatz and Dalvi [9] in- vestigated benzocaine permeation in hairless mouse skin from a range of solvents and showed that, with water as a standard, interaction effects were not significant when PG was used as a solvent. Hoelgaard and Mollgaard [10] examined the effects of PG on metronidazole flux en- hancement in human skin and suggested a possible ‘car- rier-solvent’ effect. Kasting et al. [11] demonstrated sig- nificant permeation enhancement of triprolidine base from pure PG when compared with oil vehicles in human skin. These workers related this direct effect of PG on the membrane to solvent incorporation in the stratum cor- neum in a sufficient quantity to modify permeant solu- bility in the tissue. As demonstrated previously, topical presentations of agents like ibuprofen which are intended for subcutane- ous action cannot be assumed to be pharmaceutically and clinically equivalent or indeed interchangeable [12] . Moreover, although it is evident that different penetra- tion enhancers influence drug diffusion characteristics to different extents [13] , the complexity of most pharma- ceutical preparations makes it difficult to deconvolve the exact influence of specific enhancers on drug perme- ation. Recently we reported the results of a systematic in- vestigation of the influence of binary systems of ethanol and water on ibuprofen solubility and in vitro permeation characteristics in model membranes and human skin [14] . Analysis of the data confirmed that increasing etha- nol content enhanced the solubility of ibuprofen in both membranes as well as in the vehicle itself. The aim of this work was to extend these studies to explore the influence of PG on ibuprofen permeation and to investigate wheth- er specific effects on partition and diffusion parameters may be distinguished. A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems.
